result:
On the cusp of dusk, when the last commuter train sighs across the iron bridge, I think the city exhales carbon and longing in equal measure. Streetlights bloom like cautious flowers, replacing the sun with a chlorophyll of dreams. Somewhere below, a pawnbroker turns his brass clock backward an hour, believing he can trade regret for repossession; the watch hands obey him only to resume their forward crawl once the front door is locked.  

Across the canal, a teenager with earphones the size of nostalgia rewinds the same three songs until their chords carry the exact tremolo of his parents' divorcing silence. He counts crows instead of stars because there are always more wings than wishes. When a black-feathered bird lands on the rusted crane above him, the beak clacks out Morse neither of us ever learned. We pretend it says, “Whatever you leave behind is compost, not ruin.”  

Meanwhile, I pocket the receipt from the 24-hour bakery, still warm with butter ghosts and yesterday’s dough. Ink fades in real time, proof that even proofs forget. I tear half to start tomorrow’s grocery list, half to fold a paper boat for the canal. It floats ten brave centimeters before surrendering to its own soluble heart.  

Tomorrow the teenager will switch playlists, the pawnbroker will lose another minute, and I will stand again on this bridge, ankles leaning like italics into the version of the city that will let itself be replaced overnight yet insist it is unchanged.

Across the canal, a teenager with earphones the size of nostalgia rewinds the same three songs until their chords carry the exact tremolo of his parents' divorcing silence. He counts crows instead of stars because there are always more wings than wishes. When a black-feathered bird lands on the rusted crane above him, the beak clacks out Morse neither of us ever learned. We pretend it says, “Whatever you leave behind is compost, not ruin.”  

Meanwhile, I pocket the receipt from the 24-hour bakery, still warm with butter ghosts and yesterday’s dough. Ink fades in real time, proof that even proofs forget. I tear half to start tomorrow’s grocery list, half to fold a paper boat for the canal. It floats ten brave centimeters before surrendering to its own soluble heart.  

Tomorrow the teenager will switch playlists, the pawnbroker will lose another minute, and I will stand again on this bridge, ankles leaning like italics into the version of the city that will let itself be replaced overnight yet insist it is unchanged.
